entry.title: Re: Simplifying identification and implementation of notes for statutory reporting
entry.content:
Thanks for the query @smanda 

As of September 2021, only the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance license is offered. This gives users the flexibility to activate the licensed features at a country level and yes, for customers interested in only statutory reporting (Formerly ACR) capabilities, there is absolutely no stopping them from only using the desired subset of features and potentially choose to use the Electronic document processing capabilities at a future stage. 

As for an On premise implementation for standard functionality of statutory reporting, colleagues can refer to the step by step instructions in the Getting Started section - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/3cc1da1e1e364fadb9b1af653a9597d2/2f6fb6023eeb41e4b3bb39162eadda97.html
In terms of skills required, this is heavily dependent on the scope of the countries and reports. In general,  understanding of Odata service activations, IAM and role configurations and exposure to Attachment service and content server setup is needed. Fiori and ABAP skills would be required specifically for extensibility capabilities. FI skills would be helpful for the respective configuration of country reports but isn't a pre-requisite for statutory reporting setup in general. 

Regards, 
Anoochan

Yes Oliver, advanced story. The classic is/was not really usable in my opinion :).

Outline --> page --> context menu --> script 

The only con is, that if you have multiple pages and it is possible to jump into multiple pages from multiple sources (into 'this' story), you will need to duplicate the code in the OnInit / OnActive for ach target-jump page. If you have one page only, or you jump always into one page (start the of the story is the one page), you have no problems at all.

entry.title: Ultimate Blog Series: SAP LogServ Integration with Microsoft Sentinel
entry.content:
TLDR; let me deploy already (scroll down there to the prerequisites section)! 

Welcome to the landing page of this blog series on the SAP LogServ solution in Microsoft Sentinel. Find all the things you always wanted to know and more.

 

Beyond Application Monitoring: Complete SAP RISE Visibility

Running RISE with SAP, S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or SAP Cloud ERP private? Microsoft Sentinel by itself already delivers powerful SAP application-layer monitoring – tracking SAP user activity, business transactions, and critical events while correlating them with threat signals across your entire IT estate. That's just the beginning.

SAP LogServ is an optional service in your SAP Cloud ERP private package that unlocks access to all logs from SAP's managed services. It takes your security posture to the next level by extending Sentinel's reach deep into your infrastructure managed by SAP. Think complete HANA database insights, system-level security telemetry, and audit trails - all flowing seamlessly into your existing security operations workflow.

The result: Your security team finally gets full visibility into the managed SAP stack, from business logic to infra to database layer.

What This Series Covers

Comprehensive guidance from planning your LogServ + Sentinel deployment, log selection, customizing the solution, to advanced threat protection. Blogs are co-authored by SAP and Microsoft engineering.

 

Part

Topic

Limited Preview Announcement

Initial introduction to the solution and integration between LogServ and Microsoft Sentinel

General Availability Announcement

Deployment overview with Step-by-step SAP LogServ connector setup in Sentinel, insights on prerequisites

Part 1: Microsoft Sentinel for SAP goes agentless

SAP ERP Application layer integration with agentless data connector in Sentinel Solution for SAP

Part 2: How to customize your SAP LogServ solution in Microsoft Sentinel

Understand log types available in LogServ (volume, cost, threat protection value, etc); how to filter logs, and how to customize the solution in Sentinel

Part 3: Deploy built-in detection rules and extend to your needs (coming soon)

See the already available analytic rules shipped by SAP, and discover how to craft your own based on your needs

 

Target audiences: SAP Basis admins, security architects, SOC analysts, and compliance teams looking to enhance their SAP monitoring capabilities.

 

 

Architecture Overview

Reference diagrams for planning your implementation

Detailed Component Architecture

 

High-Level Integration Flow across the whole stack

 

Ready to transform your SAP Cloud ERP private security posture? Let's get started from here.

Which logs from RISE do you need most? Let me know in the comments or reach out directly.

Cheers Martin and Hemanth

entry.title: Re: Allowing SNOTE implementations without modification r...
entry.content:
Hi ,

I had a similar situation. We restricted S_DEVELOP and ended up having restrictions on NOTE implementation.  What I found out is that the decision for the SSCR keys popup (in ECC ) comes from the FM TRINT_CHECK_LINCENSE_EXCEPTION.

So I was looking to have a apply a similar behavior to S_DEVELOP. Didn't help much because there are too many conditions and object types combination.

Eventually, we planned to remove the conditions from S_DEVELOP , allow all uses to edit. We implemented the BADI  CTS_REQUEST_CHECK  which is triggered at TR release. Here we check ( using FM TRINT_CHECK_LINCENSE_EXCEPTION ) if any standard modifications was done. If so, the TR release is stopped with a message.

We created a new Z transaction from which the TR could be released and restricted the access to specific users in each team who act as reviewers ( and avoiding repeated request to basis to whitelist objects ).

Yes, it is a shot implementation and met the objective of having localized control in each team whenever a standard modification is being done.

entry.title: Enhancing Efficiency with SAP Job Scheduling Service and SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
What's New for SAP Business Technology Platform has recently announced the integration between the SAP Job Scheduling service and SAP Cloud ALM, designed to streamline operations and enhance efficiency.

This integration allows for seamless management and monitoring of scheduled jobs, providing a more cohesive experience by letting you track the status of your job processes directly from the UI of SAP Cloud ALM.

What Is SAP Job Scheduling Service?

SAP Job Scheduling service is a cloud-based tool that runs on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), both in the Cloud Foundry and Kyma environments. It enables you to automate and manage job scheduling across various applications. It helps in optimizing resource allocation and ensuring that tasks are executed at the right time, thereby improving operational efficiency.

For more information, refer to:

  • What Is SAP Job Scheduling Service?
  • SAP Job Scheduling Service at SAP Discovery Center 

Integration with SAP Cloud ALM

SAP Cloud ALM is a cloud-based application lifecycle management solution that helps you streamline the implementation and operation of your cloud and hybrid business solutions.

The integration with SAP Job Scheduling service allows you to monitor job execution directly within the SAP Cloud ALM environment. This means you can now track job statuses, view logs, and receive alerts, all from a single interface, reducing the need to switch between different platforms.

For more information about the integration and how you can enable it, refer to:

  • Integration with SAP Cloud ALM
  • Job & Automation Monitoring Setup & Configuration

Your Opinion Matters

Let us know what you think about the integration with SAP Cloud ALM. We would love to learn more about your experience with it so that we can make your job scheduling journey as smooth as possible.

We are also committed to enhancing the overall user experience of the SAP Job Scheduling service. To achieve this, we invite you to participate in a feedback survey accessible via the Job Scheduling service dashboard. Your insights are invaluable in helping us improve the look and feel of the dashboard, ensuring it meets your needs and expectations.

To leave us feedback via the UI of the dashboard, just choose Join our user survey!

Additionally, if you want to give us feedback on the quality of the documentation on SAP Help Portal, check out the Participate in the Documentation Feedback Survey for SAP Job Scheduling Service blog post to learn more.

We look forward to hearing from you and thank you for your continued support.

Also, feel free to share your thoughts and experiences in the comments below. Your input is always appreciated!

entry.title: Issue with SQL Query Result Differ From Manual Table Execution - EKPO & EKBE
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I’m facing an issue with a SQL query in an ABAP report that uses a JOIN and a SUM aggregation.

Example Scenario:

For a specific matnr (material number) and werks (plant), along with the other conditions, the expected SUM of quantity is 30. But the query during execution returns 120.

Problem:

When I manually pass the inputs in table level I get the correct result. However, when the same inputs are passed in query and is executed during runtime with the values passed through variables, it returns incorrect results.

Strangely, this issue occurs only in the PRD system – in DEV, it works as expected.

Here is the query I’m using:

SELECT SUM( CASE  WHEN _ekbe~shkzg = 'H'
                          THEN _ekbe~menge * ( -1 )
                          ELSE  _ekbe~menge
                   END )
       FROM ekpo AS _ekpo
       INNER JOIN ekbe AS _ekbe
         ON _ekbe~matnr EQ _ekpo~matnr
         AND _ekbe~ebeln EQ _ekpo~ebeln
         AND _ekbe~bwart IS NOT INITIAL
       WHERE _ekpo~matnr = @meprnitem-matnr
       AND _ekpo~werks = @meprnitem-werks
       AND _ekpo~elikz IS INITIAL
       AND _ekpo~loekz IS INITIAL
       INTO @LV_quant_ekbe.

entry.title: Netting in Accounts Receivable and Accounts Payable in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
In this blog post, I would like to cover a small yet useful functionality in the Accounts Payable (AP) module of SAP S/4HANA: Netting of Accounts Receivable (AR) and Accounts Payable (AP). While researching this topic online, I found limited end-to-end documentation, so this blog is aimed at filling that gap with a clear walkthrough.

Scope of the Blog

  • Create a Business Partner and extend it to Supplier (FI) and Customer (FI) roles

  • Create AR and AP invoices

  • Execute netting using the Automatic Payment Program (APP)

1. Create a Business Partner and Extend It to Customer and Supplier Roles

First, create a Business Partner (BP) who will act as both a supplier and a customer. To do this, use the "Maintain Business Partner" app.

  • Select "Organization" as the BP type.

  • In the "Business Partner (Gen.)" role, go to the "Payment Transactions" tab and maintain the Bank Details (Bank Key, Account Number, IBAN, etc.) as shown in the screenshot below:

Next, extend the same BP to the following roles:

  • Customer (FI)

  • Supplier (FI)

Within the Company Code section of each role, ensure the following:

  • In the Payment Transactions tab, specify a Payment Method that will later be used in the APP.

  • Tick the "Clearing with Vendor"/"Clearing with Customer" checkbox. This setting is critical for enabling netting between AR and AP.

Finally, under the Supplier (FI) role, make sure to fill in the Customer Number in the General Data section to establish the link between the customer and supplier accounts:

2. Create AR and AP Invoices

Now, create AR and AP invoices using the customer and supplier accounts created in the previous step. A few important points to remember:

  • Leave the Payment Method field empty in both documents. If it is filled, the APP will treat them as separate payments, defeating the purpose of netting.

  • Ensure that the total payable amount is greater than the receivable, so that the APP will generate a net payment for the difference.

This ensures that the system can offset the AR and AP balances during payment processing.

3. Schedule the Automatic Payment Program (APP)

Use the "Manage Automatic Payments" app to execute the netting process:

  • Create a new parameter run and enter the relevant Customer/Supplier number, Company Code, and Payment Method.

  • Ensure the invoices are due on the payment run date.

  • Run the Payment Proposal and check the results.

If everything is configured correctly, you will see a single payment that offsets both open items and pays only the balance.

Conclusion

This simple configuration allows you to reduce unnecessary cash flows and administrative efforts by settling AR and AP balances for the same business partner via netting. Although small, this functionality can be very useful in real-world business scenarios—especially when dealing with intercompany transactions or recurring vendors who are also customers.

Regards,
Rakhmatilla Tuganov

entry.title: Part 1- GST Implementation Steps for Materials procurement for Deductible & Non-Deductible Taxes.
entry.content:
GST Implementation Steps for Materials for Deductible & Non-Deductible Taxes (Intra State & Inter State)

The GST Implementing Steps for Material Procurement for both Intrastate & Interstate Deductible & Intrastate & Interstate Non-Deductible scenarios.

The Deductible & Non Deductible concept is as below –

Let us see how do we configure the same in SAP S4HANA.

Step 1: Create Condition Tables

Tcode M/03

Create a new condition Table 793 (You can create you own 3 digit number apart from SAP reserved numbers)  with the following combination

In the Technical View make the following fields as Item Fields.

Step 2: Assignment of condition table into Access Sequence- Tcode: OBQ2

Assign the newly created Table into existing access sequence JGSI.

Insert the table 793 into the access sequence JGSI:

 

Check the fields under Access’s Table: 793

If any field is missing then press the field catalogue and select the field from the catalogue and choose the corresponding field either from the header/Item as per the condition record. In the case below LLAND is defined as a header field so choose LLAND from KOMK from the field catalogue.

KOMK is the header table and KOMP is the item table.

Region of Dlv Plant is to be me made as KOMP – WKREG

Vendor Classification is KOMP and has to be initialized.

Change the “Region of Dlv. Plant” Condition Field’s Document structure from KOMK to KOMP

Ref:  SAP Note 2407980 where WKREG is to be assigned to KOMP.

Step 3: Assignment of Access Sequence to condition types.

Prepare MM Condition types JICG, JISG resembling CGST & SGST for deductible Procurement.

Condition type JIIG resembling IGST for deductible procurement.

Condition types JICN, JISN resembling CGST & SGST for non deductible Procurement.

Condition type JIIN resembling IGST for non deductible procurement.

Tcode: OBQ1

Deductible Condition Types –  JICG, JISG, JIIG

Copy from SAP Standard Condition type MWVS

Condition Type : JISG - IN: State GST and assign JGSI access sequence.

Condition Type : JICG - IN: Central GST and assign JGSI access sequence.

Condition Type : JIIG - IN: Integrated GST and assign JGSI access sequence.

Non- Deductible Condition Types –  JICN, JISN, JIIN

Condition Type : JICN - IN: Central GST-ND and assign JGSI access sequence.

Condition Type : JISN - IN: State GST-ND and assign JGSI access sequence

Condition Type : JIIN - IN: Integrated GST-ND and assign JGSI access sequence.

Step 4: Tax Procedure creation:

Tcode: OBQ3

Step 5: Assignment of Tax condition types to Tax Pricing Procedure

Step 6: Assignment of Tax Procedure to Company Code:

Tcode: OBBG

Step 7: Creation of Tax Codes:

Deductible Tax Code created IP

 

Non-Deductible Tax Code created I0

Step 8: Account Key to be assigned to GL in OB40

Account Key JIC for JICG

Account key JIS for State SGST

Account Key JII – JIIG (Integrated).

Step 9: Creation of Chapter ID’s

Tcode: J1ID

Step 10: Extension of Chapter ID to Material Code:

Step 11: Set Tax Indicator for Material: Tcode OMKK

Step 12: Extension of Tax Category to Material Code:

Step 13: Condition Record Maintenance –

Tcode FV11 –

Case 1: Within the State Material procurement & Deductible.

 Tax code to be used IP.

Condition Type: JISG – 2.5%

Condition Type  JICG 2.5 %

Scenario Test: Within the State Procurement of Material–

Purchase Order with Tax Code IP

Tax Calculation in PO –

GRN – No impact of taxes on Inventory during GRN

Stock Account Dr +

GR/IR Clearing Cr-

Invoice Booking –

Vendor Acc Cr –

GR/IR Clearing Dr+

CGST & SGST Dr +

Case 2: Inter State Material procurement & Deductible

Tax code to be used IP.

Condition Type: JIIG –

 

Scenario Test: Outside the State procurement.

Purchase Order with Tax Code IP

 

GRN:

 

Stock Account DR +

GR/IR Clearing Account CR –

MIRO:

 

Vendor Acc Cr –

GR/IR Clearing Account DR +

IGST Input DR+ 

Case 3:  Within the State Material procurement & Non-Deductible.

Tax code to be used I0.

Condition Type: JISN – 2.5%

Condition Type: JICN – 2.5%

Scenario Test: Within the State

Purchase Order with Tax Code I0

GRN –

Stock Account DR+

GR/IR Clearing CR-

The Tax amount is added into the Inventory cost.

MIRO:

Vendor Account Cr-

Case 4: Inter State Material procurement & Non-Deductible.

Condition Type: JIIN – 12%

 

GRN:

GR/IR Clearing CR-

Regards,

Sagnik.

entry.title: Re: ewm-qm integration stock type problem
entry.content:
Hi Melike,

Depends if you work with inspection rules or with the material master, but you could have a look at the material/inspection rule setting for 'Control Lot Creation' which should have the value 'for each material document item' instead '1 for each material document, material, batch storage loc'.

Other less likely causses could be that you have a SKIP lot, have not check 'post to quality stock' in the material master, that the inspection rule is not directing to quality stock (independent stock type).

 

Hope this gives some direction in your search to the cause.

 

greetings, Herman

entry.title: Re: use case for "Application.getfiledatasource" ?
entry.content:
Hi @sv7en,
please note that you have:

  • a Model Name (=technical name), like SAP_GRL_RL_EmissionComparisonKPI or TITECON_Demo_Model
  • a Model Description, like "Emmision Comparison KPI" or "Demo Model"
  • a Model ID, like "Cu4o1l0eqjiphse1tosogajc2u"

The first ones are the ones you usually see if you e.g. use the files browser in SAC. The model ID you can e.g. get by:

  • Using the Data Analyzer on the SAC Standard Model "RESOURCE": There you filter on the Model Name and output the Model ID. This method is especially helpful, if you´d like to get the IDs of several Models
  • Using coding. If you´re already coding anyways, you can just use this method.

Let´s dive into the coding option and let´s assume you have a Table named "P1_TAB_Demo", connected to a model (in my case with the name "TITECON_Demo_Model"). Now create a button with an onClick Script event:

console.log (P1_TAB_Demo.getDataSource().getInfo());

This will result can be shown in the browser developer tools console (F12):

So in my Example the model ID output is "t.8:Ca0dju0b3o8jdb3sh78rh61a35r", where "t.8" is the Content Namespace of the SAC tenant, which is not relevant, so the model ID you should use in your further coding is only "Ca0dju0b3o8jdb3sh78rh61a35r". 

Just hit me directly, if you need any further assistance.

Best regards,
Denis

entry.title: Re: Reasons for Rejection is error in the report
entry.content:
Hi,

1. The Rejection Reason is not a leading dimension in the report. The Sales Order ID is the leading dimension. 

2. By setting the Rejection Reason in Sales Order, it does not affect the outbound delivery. Therefore, the Rejection Reason applied to the Sales Order Item 1 with Quantity 10, instead of 5. 

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.content:
Hi @Yogananda 

Thank you for your prompt response.

I have a follow-up question regarding the retrieval of Base64-encoded/binary content of a document.

I attempted to call the document service API using the document ID as follows:
https://myXXXXXX.de1.test.crm.cloud.sap/sap/c4c/api/v1/case-service/cases/{document-id}

However, the response payload does not appear to include the binary content of the document. Am I missing a specific parameter or endpoint?

In Service Cloud V1, we were able to retrieve the binary (Base64) data directly in the response. This allowed us to perform a conversion to actual document with the help of systems such as SAP CPI or any third-party system.

We are trying to achieve similar functionality in V2, but currently do not see the binary content included in the response.

Could you please advise if there's a separate endpoint or method to retrieve the binary data in V2? Any guidance or clarification would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks - Manson

entry.title: Re: Fright Charge for Customers
entry.content:
Hi,

Many organizations, including distributors, have specific strategies for handling freight charges when customer orders fall below a certain threshold value. Here are some common approaches:

1. Single Line Item Charge 04 TM Freight Cost and SD Customer Billing

This is the ideal solution for many businesses, as it simplifies billing and helps maintain transparency with customers. The freight charge is added as a separate line item on the invoice, clearly labeled, so the customer understands the cost incurred due to the order being below the stipulated value.

Steps to Implement Single Line Charge in SAP:

  • Utilize SAP Sales and Distribution (SD) module’s pricing procedure. Set up a condition type specifically for this freight charge.
  • Configure the pricing procedure to automatically apply this charge whenever the order value falls below your defined minimum.
  • Ensure that your billing document reflects this charge as an independent line item.

2. Embedded Freight Cost in Product Price

Sometimes businesses prefer not to display the freight cost separately but embed it within the product price.

  • Adjust product pricing to incorporate a fixed percentage or flat rate increment corresponding to anticipated freight costs.
  • This approach can simplify invoicing but may reduce transparency regarding actual freight costs.

3. Discount Adjustments

Implementing adjustments to discounts provided can help cover freight costs for smaller orders without adding explicit charges:

  • Revise discount levels for orders below the minimum value threshold, effectively increasing the net price to account for freight.

Additional Considerations:

  • Customer Communication: Regardless of the method chosen, clear communication with customers is crucial to manage expectations regarding freight charges.
  • System Configuration: Ensure your ERP system (like SAP) is configured to handle these adjustments automatically, minimizing manual intervention and reducing errors.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: Re: sap public cloud BADI needed for VF02 release to acco...
entry.content:
Hi,

To address your requirement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud for integrating and triggering BAdIs during accounting document creation or release while using VF01 or VF02, you may want to consider several available Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) specifically designed for sales billing and related processes. Although the specific BAdIs you mentioned are not triggering, there are other BAdIs available that might provide a solution for your need.

Suggested BAdIs for Sales Billing:

  1. Custom Data Transfer for Billing Documents (SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER): Sales - Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) for Sales Billing

    • Business Context: Sales: Billing Document Item
    • Use: This BAdI allows you to create custom data transfer routines to control how the system copies data from reference documents to billing documents, which might be useful for integrating custom tables during document creation.

  2. Header Modification in Standard Output Form for Billing Documents (SD_BIL_PRINT_STANDARD_HEAD): Sales - Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) for Sales Billing

    • Business Context: Sales: Billing Document
    • Use: This BAdI modifies custom header fields in the standard output form for billing documents. While its primary use is for print forms, it might provide insights on enhancing billing document handling.

  3. Modification of Custom Header Fields in Customer Invoice SOAP Message (SD_BIL_EDI_INV_REQ_HEAD): Sales - Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) for Sales Billing

    • Business Context: Sales: Billing Document
    • Use: This BAdI can be used to modify values in custom header fields during invoicing, potentially allowing integration with custom table updates.

Best regards, Dequan Xu

entry.title: Day 8/30 – Material Requirements Planning (MRP) in SAP PP
entry.content:
Time to step into the engine room of planning — MRP!
Material Requirements Planning is the core function that ensures you have the right materials, in the right quantity, at the right time — without overstocking or risking shortages.

🔍 What is MRP?
MRP analyzes demand (planned orders, sales orders, forecasts) and supply (stock, purchase orders, production orders) to create procurement or production proposals.

It answers:
➡️ What materials are needed?
➡️ How much is needed?
➡️ When is it needed?

⚙️ Types of MRP in SAP PP:
🔹 MRP Run (Classic MRP) – Transaction: MD01, MD02, MD03
🔹 MRP Live (S/4HANA) – Transaction: MD01N (real-time, in-memory)
🔹 Net Change vs. Regenerative Planning
🔹 Lot sizing, Safety stock, Procurement type — all play a role

🚀 What does MRP generate?

  • Planned Orders (for in-house production)

  • Purchase Requisitions (for external procurement)

  • Scheduling info for production and procurement

  • Exception messages (e.g. rescheduling alerts)

✅ Pro Tip:
Fine-tune MRP parameters in the Material Master (MRP1–MRP4 views). A small mistake in lot sizing or procurement type can ripple through your entire planning process!

Generated by AI with my custom prompt

💬 Have you transitioned to MRP Live yet? Or are you still running classic batch-based MRP? Drop your insights below!

#SAP #SAPPP #MRP #MaterialPlanning #ProductionPlanning #ERP #SAPS4HANA #MRPLive #SmartManufacturing #Manufacturing #DigitalTransformation #LinkedInSeries #SupplyChain #InventoryManagement

entry.title: Quantity Contract & Scheduling Agreement in SAP SD
entry.content:
Introduction:
In this Blog, we will take a deep dive into Contract Types in SAP SD, covering their significance, configurations, and real-time business applications. In SAP Sales and Distribution (SD), contracts play a crucial role in long-term business agreements between a
company and its customers. SAP SD offers different contract types, each catering to specific business needs. These include:
❖ Quantity Contract.
❖ Scheduling agreement.                                                                       

                                                                                               Quantity Contract:

Interpretation: It is a long-term agreement between a customer and a company for supplying goods of Particular Quantity within a specific validity period of time.
Business Scenario: Let’s Assume a customer commits to purchase 500 laptops and 200 Desktops from HP in a year, customer places sales order as much as till the target quality is referenced.
Configuration Steps:
Sales organization =HLSO, distribution channel =01, Division =01
Here Sales Document type = CQ.
Define sales document type in VOV8 and assign it to sales area using OVAZ
Item Category =KMN.
Define item category in VOV7 and do item category determination in VOV4 KMN = CQ+NORM
Scheduled lines = CN
Define schedule lines in VOV6 and do schedule Line determination in VOV5 CN = KMN+ND
Create Quantity contract using T-code VA41.

                                                             

 

 

 

 

                                      

Now Save it.

Create sales order in VA01 with reference to Contract, followed by delivery and Billing.

Click on Create with Reference.

Go to Contract tab and Contract Number and Click on Copy.

Go to VA43 to observe How much quantity is used out of target Quantity

In the Above Screenshot , sales order has been created with 20 quantities for item 10 and an additional 20 quantities for item 20 and the system still allows the creation of sales orders with 30 quantities for item 10 and 10 quantities for item 20. Also, if we observe Overall Status is In Process which implies there still Order Quantity Leftover to create Sales Order’s w.r.to Quantity Contract.

Again, Trying to Create sales order with Reference to Quantity contract.

Now Again Go to VA43 to observe How much quantity is used out of target Quantity Here, we can Observe that Contract is Fully Referenced and Overall Status is Completed.

Go to VA43 and click on Document flow Icon, to see how many sales orders were created against this quantity Contract and their status

As we observe, in the Document there are 2 Sales Orders have been Created as shown above and Overall Quantity Contract Status is Completed, since w.r.to Quantity Contract, Order Quantity have reached the limit.

                                                                                 Scheduled Line Agreement:

Interpretation: Scheduled Line Agreement is an agreement between a customer and an organization for supplying goods with predefined confirmed quantity and delivery dates.

Business Scenario: A customer from Walmart gets into a Scheduling Agreement with Apple for 600 MacBook Pros over the next six months  with deliveries of laptops on : 03/25/2025 of 150 quantities with deliveries of laptops on 05/24/2025 of 250 quantities with deliveries of laptops on 07/22/2025 of 200 quantities.

Configuration Steps:

Let’s say, Sales organization =HLSO, distribution channel =01, Division =01

Sales Document type = DS 

Define sales document type in VOV8 and assign it to the sales area using OVAZ

Item Category =LPN

Define item category in VOV7 and do item category determination in VOV4 LPN=DS+NORM

Scheduled lines = CN 

Defining scheduled lines in VOV6 and do schedule line determination in VOV5

CN=LPN+ND 

Assign Delivery Document Type and Billing document Type in VOV8.

Create Scheduling Agreement using T-code VA31 and go to Schedule lines Tab and Maintain Confirmed Quantity and Delivery Dates.

Click on Material.

Go to Schedule Line Tab and Fill the Details as per the Schedule Line Agreement.

After creating a Scheduling Agreement using transaction VA31, you can proceed to create deliveries with reference to the agreement.

  • When creating deliveries, it is crucial to ensure that the delivery date selected is not earlier than the scheduled line-item dates maintained in the scheduling agreement.
  • The system will only allow delivery creation based on the confirmed quantities and their respective confirmed delivery dates.

For example, consider the following scheduled delivery dates and quantities:

April 25, 2025 – 4 unit , May 15, 2025 – 3 units , June 01, 2025 – 3 units

  • If you try to create a delivery with a date earlier than the scheduled line dates, the system will not allow it unless the quantities have already been confirmed.
  • If you provide a delivery date of May 15, 2025, the system allows you to create a delivery for the April 25 and May 15 schedule lines, assuming the quantities are confirmed and still open.
  • However, if you select the last confirmed delivery date (e.g., June 01, 2025), the system can allow the creation of a delivery including quantities from earlier confirmed dates (like March and May), assuming they are still open for delivery and not yet fulfilled.

Make sure to give Relevant Schedule lines to create delivery otherwise system throws below Error.

Create Deliveries with Reference to Scheduling Agreement in VL01N.

Now Create the delivery with 1st Schedule Line:

Now Create the delivery with 2nd Schedule Line:

Now Create the delivery with 3rd Schedule Line:

To monitor the status of a Scheduling Agreement and the deliveries created with reference to it, use transaction VA33 and click on Document Flow to view the complete history and status.

After creating the delivery, perform picking for each delivery document using VL02N by entering the picked quantity.

Once picking is completed, execute Post Goods Issue (PGI) to confirm that the goods have left the warehouse.

After PGI, create the billing document using VF01 with reference to the delivery to generate the invoice.

                                                                                     Conclusion:

Quantity/Value Contracts → You create a release order (i.e., sales order) referencing the contract.

Scheduling Agreements → You don’t create separate sales orders; instead, you maintain delivery schedules directly in the scheduling agreement.

entry.title: Re: ewm-qm integration stock type problem
entry.content:
Hello Herman,

Thanks for your comments. However, in our case, there is no inspection rule in use only the material master is configured with inspection type 02. This setup is fine for us, as we expect one inspection lot for the same product and batch, which is the desired behavior.

The issue is that stock posting is inconsistent.
In some cases, the stock is correctly posted to quality inspection (Q), but in other cases, it is posted to unrestricted-use (F) during goods receipt  even though there is no dynamic modification rule maintained.

entry.title: Understanding HANA memory related license utilization in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
When using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, your license usage depends on several factors. These include counts of different user types, business objects, service calls, or how much you use system resources. In this blog, we'll focus on the last factor: resource usage.

Specifically, we'll look at how memory-related license metrics are calculated and answer some common questions about it.

The way memory usage is measured in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition has evolved, and there are now three different methods co-existing: C414 for 2-System Landscape (2SL), and CY263 or CY500 for 3-System Landscape (3SL). The method that applies to you depends on your contract. This blog will explain all three methods, starting with general information that applies to all of them, and then diving into the specifics of each.

Fig. 1: Example of memory utilization metric in license dashboard

It is not easy to get transparency on the reasons for growing memory license consumption. Therefore we will discuss the available options to identify them in a separate blog. And in another blog, we will go over the existing tools for reducing data .

Introduction

License consumption must-knows

No matter which license is relevant to your system, the following must be known before reading the part relevant to your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system:

  • License utilization details are available in SAP for Me. See further information here: License Utilization Information.
  • The “entitlement” also known as “subscription” is the contractual maximum of the license. We use the term “entitlement” in this blog.
  • The “consumption” also referred to as “measurement” is the current usage of the entitlement.
  • The memory entitlement is checked against the production system only. The sizes of other systems in your landscape such as Test, Dev or Quality systems are not considered, i.e. the size of the non-productive systems is not added up. In extreme cases, the memory consumption of the test system may be larger than the production one (e.g. shortly before go-live) and this situation will not affect the memory related license metric.
  • If the consumption is above the entitlement, e.g. due to growing data volumes or intensive usage of the system, SAP will continue to provide the required memory to prevent performance problems. At this point, either a reduction of consumption must be made, or an extension of the entitlement must be acquired.
  • All memory-related license metrics are based on SAP HANA database memory. Memory requirements or usage for other parts of your SAP S/4HANA landscape, such as the ABAP Application Servers, are not included in these metrics.
  • The unit used in license measurement is a Gigabyte (GB). 1 GB = 1.000.000.000 bytes.

Main components of the HANA database from a license perspective

In this blog, the following picture will be used to explain the differences between the various consumption metrics.  

Fig 2.: Simplified view of SAP HANA components relevant for license consumption

This graphic illustrates disk consumption in the bottom part (grey) and memory consumption in the upper part (blue and green).

The memory (top) part has the following components:

  • Used Memory - Data Footprint: (dark blue color) Data loaded from HANA disk to HANA memory.
  • Used Memory - Processing Memory: (light blue color) HANA memory is not only used to have the data available for fast access but is also required for management of the HANA DB and for processing queries, e.g. storing intermediate results or aggregations. This component can be as large as the sum of the data components (aka data footprint) in memory.
  • Allocated Memory: (light green) This is memory that is still free but already reserved (i.e. allocated) for this database to allow future growth or peak load during upgrades. It is usually between 30 and 50% of the total capacity provided.
  • Available Memory: This is memory that could be added to the database dynamically if required.

The data footprint consists of the following 3 types of data:

  • Cold Data: In this context, LOB data: Large Objects. Typically, ABAP coding or large interface messages such as xml, JSON documents or job spool requests, ... It is usually read once and hardly ever again. It has a disk to memory ratio larger than 20 to 1.
  • Warm Data: In this context, NSE data: Native Storage Extension. Typically, this is all sort of log data, such as change documents, application log, security audit log… This data is rarely read. It has a disk to memory ratio of 5 to 1.
  • Hot Data: In this context, this is Column and Row Store Data. Mostly business data that is read, used, changed and created by the users when using apps, by background jobs, and by API calls. It has a disk to memory ratio close to 1 to 1.

Note that there is no aging mechanism to move data from cold to warm or hot or reverse. Data is created as hot, warm or cold by definition. This distinction is made only in this blog for explanatory purposes as the 'data temperature' can play a role whether data is included in the license metric or not.

And there is an additional dimension to the data components described above:

  • SAP data: Essential part of the system that is completely independent of any business activity. For example, the UI5 and ABAP code, apps metadata, in-app help texts and their translations, object definitions, system logs... The size and the content of this data cannot be influenced by SAP customers. SAP decides which data is stored and for how long.
  • Customer data - also known as Customer Business Data: Data populated by SAP customers’ business processes. The data is ‘owned’ by SAP’s customers, and it is their responsibility to decide how this data should be handled, e.g. if and when this data should be archived or deleted (i.e. retention management).

How does SAP distinguish between SAP data and Customer data?

Historically, one SAP S/4HANA installation for On-Premises customers could be used by different companies as the data model includes a field to allow data segregation. The so-called ‘client’ or ‘mandant’ field is part of most tables. The ‘customer owned data’ is the sum of the data in the customer client (100), i.e. your productive working client.

Some tables might have both customer and SAP data (For example, data in client 000 and client 100). In such case, the license measurement (for C414 and CY500) counts the entries and proportionally calculates the size of both. Tables without a client field are SAP owned.

FAQ: What about attachments and outputs?

Outputs or GOS attachments and DMS documents, such as PDF or MS-Word or MS-Excel, are not stored in the SAP HANA database and therefore are not considered in the licenses discussed in this blog. Instead, they are stored in the BTP Document Management Service (DMS) via an SAP managed communication scenario. Alternatively, it is possible to connect a CMIS certified repository of choice, e.g. for sharing a companywide repository across different solutions via SAP_COM_0597.

Note, to increase the speed of creating output documents, e.g. printouts of billing documents, these are temporarily stored as LOB data (Cold Data on the picture) within the SAP HANA database and are offloaded to the external storage repository several times a day, so they don’t have a relevant impact on memory usage.

2-System Landscape - (Metric ID C414)

The metric you can see in your licensing dashboard for a 2-system landscape (2SL) system (C414) represents the disk size of customer owned Warm and Hot Data.

The licensing metric displayed in SAP for Me is kept stable by excluding the two highest consumption peak values of the past 30 days.

Measurement scope

Fig 3. 2-system landscape - components included in license metric C414

C414 advantages:

  • The “SAP Data” is not part of the consumption measurement.
  • Cold Data is not part of the consumption measurement.

C414 disadvantages:

  • The complete size of the Warm Data is part of the consumption measurement. No matter if only a small portion of it is loaded to memory.

The minimum entitlement of C414 is 100 GB and can be larger if system extension or more FUEs are purchased.

FAQ: Why is there a sudden increase in the C414 license measure in August 2024?

Due to changes in upgrade techniques, over time, more and more data became no longer visible to the C414 license measurement. It was misclassified as SAP Data. With 2408, a correction was implemented that made this data visible again. This means the August peak you might see in your license measurement is not reflecting a sudden data growth but instead made visible a data growth that existed for many months or years.

3-System Landscape – (Metric ID CY263)

The metric you can see in your licensing dashboard for 3-system landscape (3SL) systems (CY263) represents the total HANA memory provided to your tenant to ensure that the tenant can be operated without memory shortages. Technically, this is the “indexserver Process Allocation Limit” or PAL.

The licensing metric displayed in SAP for Me is kept stable by excluding the two highest consumption peak values of the past 30 days.

Measurement scope

The measurement includes:

  • Cold, Warm and Hot Data memory footprint
  • Processing memory
  • Free memory

The reason for changing the approach from ‘data footprint-based’ (2-system landscape C414) to ‘full-memory-usage’ (3-system landscape CY263) was the increased options for system extensibility. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Environment, it is possible for SAP customers to define their own custom objects. Execution of complex queries on custom CDS views may lead to a significant and unpredictable consumption of the processing memory of the SAP HANA database. To reflect this in the license consumption, this part of the HANA memory was added to the metric measurement.

Fig. 4: 3-system landscape - components included in license metric CY263

CY263 advantages:

  • The license consumption mirrors precisely the system’s cost as everything needed to operate the system is included in the measurement.
  • Only the part loaded to memory of the Cold and Warm Data is measured.

CY263 disadvantages:

  • The measurement does not start at 0. The system is provisioned with a minimum of 150 GB.
  • SAP Data and Customer Business data are not distinguished.
  • The measurement depends on SAP’s decision of how much free memory shall be allocated to the system. SAP operates the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenants in a way that the memory requirements are monitored regularly and adjusted automatically. It cannot be influenced by you as SAP customer.
  • HANA memory intensive queries on CDS views may increase the memory consumption, although the data footprint has not changed. This can be complicated to identify.

The minimum entitlement of CY263 is 200 GB and is extended if system extension or more FUEs are purchased.

FAQ: How does the CY263 measurement relate to the figures displayed in Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (Cloud ALM) Health Monitoring?

The metric ‘HANA Memory Used’ in Health Monitoring of Cloud ALM provides the current HANA memory usage (99.78 GB in this example) compared to the CY263 entitlement (200 GB in this example). The CY263 consumption is not displayed in this dashboard.

Fig. 5: Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (CALM) view on used memory

The Memory used can be compared to the sum of the blue boxes in Figure 4. (All 3 types of Data in memory and the Processing Memory). It does not include the free and available memory. And it shows the current memory and not the recent peak

3-System Landscape Starting June 2025 – (Metric ID CY500)

The metric you can see in your licensing dashboard for 3SL systems (CY500) for contracts signed after June 2025 represents the current data memory footprint of customer owned data.

The value displayed in SAP for Me is the maximum value measured every day.

Measurement scope

The measurement includes:

  • The memory usage of Customer Hot Data
  • The memory usage of Customer Warm Data
  • 5% of the disk size of Customer Cold Data

Fig. 6: 3-system landscape - components included in license metric CY500

CY500 advantages:

  • The consumption measurement only considers customer data and is more relatable to the system usage.
  • Only a fraction of the Cold Data is part of the consumption measurement. This fraction is included to take into account rare cases where Cold Data becomes extremely large.

CY500 disadvantages:

  • Processing memory is not part of the license but is nonetheless physically limited.

The minimum entitlement of CY500 is 16 GB (for 25 users) and is extended based on the number of users.

FAQ: Why is the minimum entitlement for CY500 much smaller than the one for CY263?

Around 150 GB of memory must be provided to an empty S/4HANA Public Cloud system to operate without memory shortages. This requirement is caused by the high memory usage of Near-Zero Downtime upgrades and Hotfix implementations during which a large part of the system is duplicated. CY500 does not include SAP Data and starts at zero.

With a minimum entitlement of 200 GB for CY263, the actual memory available for growth is around 50 GB. An increase of 1 GB of customer business data might mean an increase of another 1 GB of required processing memory (As described by Sizing 1793345). Therefore, the space available for customer business data is about 25 GB. And this is comparable to the minimum value of CY500.

FAQ: We archived a lot of data, why is the reduction on the license measurement smaller than expected?

There are various reasons for this:

  • CY500 and CY263 are both used to measure memory usage. If the data archived is of type Cold or Warm Data, there is a high chance that this data was not in memory but on disk. Therefore, memory usage might have been reduced much less than disk usage.

If you use the ILM Advisor app, be aware that it shows currently the object size as calculated for C414. Metric C414 measures disk usage, so archiving should have a direct impact on this metric and the values displayed in ILM Advisor app.

  • The values displayed in SAP for Me for Metrics C414 and CY263 are the maximum values over the last 30 days. Therefore, it takes 30 days to see a decrease. In between, other data might have grown and that will make the decrease visually smaller.
  • The sizes displayed in “Manage Archiving variant” show the size of the archive file. It does not have a 1-to-1 relation to the reduction of memory usage in HANA as different compression algorithms are used.
  • Depending on the archiving object, it can also be that the deletion in the SAP S/4HANA system was not done yet and will happen with the archiving of other related objects. For example archiving of controlling related archiving objects, e.g. COPA2_A000 for profitability analysis or CO_TRANS for cost-center line items do not delete records but do updates to allow a later final archiving of accounting documents (archiving object FI_DOCUMNT)

Conclusion

We are aware that it unfortunately requires quite a bit of in-depth understanding of the SAP HANA database architecture to be able to put the activities performed on your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system e.g. data archiving, posting business documents, creating logs, performing database queries in relation to the effect on the licensing. Still, we hope that this blog could provide first insights and helps with a better understanding.

We will discuss the available options to identify them in a separate blog. And in another blog, we will go over the existing tools for reducing data and will provide hands-on guidance on how to reduce commonly growing data e.g. Security Audit Logs or Application Integration Framework (AIF) data.

entry.title: 🚀 Avoid Performance Pitfalls: Master Data Design Tips for SAP Convergent Charging
entry.content:
As SAP Convergent Charging adoption grows in both B2C and B2B business models, a robust and scalable master data model has become essential. In high-volume environments, the right design can ensure performance, scalability, and system stability. Poor initial design can lead to serious performance bottlenecks that are difficult to fix post go-live.

Let’s explore why master data design is critical in SAP Convergent Charging (SAP CC) and what actions can be taken to implement an optimal setup from day one.

🔍Why Master Data Design Matters in SAP CC Implementations

SAP Convergent Charging (SAP CC) is the core pricing and charging engine in the SAP Billing and Revenue Innovation Management (BRIM) suite. As the customer base—and pricing models—become more complex, especially in B2B scenarios, a poorly structured master data model can significantly degrade performance.

💡Key Takeaway: The more complex the business hierarchy and the larger the customer base, the more critical system performance becomes.

📘Start with the SAP-Recommended Guidelines

Before you dive into system configuration, one of the most valuable resources to review is:

📝SAP Note 2478958 – Impact of the Master Data on the SAP CC Performance

This note provides benchmark values and design tips. While not hard limits, they represent best practices that have been proven across customer implementations.

⚠️Common Pitfall: Large B2B Customers 

Many organizations—especially B2B service providers—model large customers as single business partners with multiple provider contracts. This approach seems logical but often leads to system performance degradation due to high volume of provider contracts and high volume of usages.

✅Solution: Account Splitting

SAP BRIM offers a feature called Account Split, which uses a new concept of alternative subscriber account in SAP CC. 

This allows each provider contract within a large B2B business partner to be distributed equally across separate alternative subscriber accounts—without impacting master data consistency in Subscription Order Management or Convergent Invoicing/FI- Contract Accounting.

For more information on how to implement Account Split refer to Account Split on SAP Help portal.

🧱 Building a Scalable Foundation

Designing your master data model is not just a technical step—it's a strategic decision. A few recommendations to guide your design process:

📌Design Tips:

  • Use SAP Convergent Charging master data recommendations as a benchmark (from SAP Note 2478958)
  • Leverage account split early for large B2B customers
  • Perform initial system sizing based on projected data volumes and re-evaluate periodically as the business scales

🛠️ More fine-tuning

Beyond master data, other parameters and configuration settings can be optimized depending on:

  • Data volume
  • Business complexity
  • Functional requirements

👥Need Help? SAP Premium Engagement Services Are Available

Early-stage design mistakes in SAP BRIM projects can become expensive to fix later. That’s why it’s strongly recommended to engage expert services from:

🧭 SAP Premium Engagement/ MaxAttention CoE

This teams provides following services:

  • Design and architecture evaluation
  • Performance optimization
  • Go-live support

💬Final Thought: Design Early, Scale Confidently

Master data design in SAP CC is foundational—and difficult to change post go-live. Starting with a strong design aligned to SAP best practices helps ensure long-term success.

📣Call to Action

  • ✅Download and review SAP Note 2478958
  • ✅Use Account Split for the big customers
  • ✅Engage SAP Premium Engagement Services for an early review

🗣️ Has SAP CC master data design been optimized in your organization? Share the experience or tips in the comments to help others avoid common pitfalls!

entry.title: Story Performance Analysis Tool in Report Center: New in 1H 2025!
entry.content:
Overview

The recent enhancements to the People Analytics module for the 1H 2025 have proven to be highly impactful. A particularly noteworthy enhancement is the ability to assess and optimize the efficiency of Story reports.

The Story Performance Analysis Tool, accessible via Report Center, enables Story administrators to assess, monitor, measure and optimize the efficiency of Story reports and captures detailed performance metrics, helping identify areas for improvement and enhance overall report performance. This enhancement aims to bring greater visibility and control over the performance aspects of Stories.

Benefits of Story Performance Analysis Tool: -

The Story Performance Analysis Tool delivers significant value to various user groups, including HR administrators, managers, and analytics power users:

  • For Administrators:

- Helps identify bottlenecks or inefficiencies in report design or data handling and minimizes report loading delays
- Allows for proactive performance tuning, such as optimizing filters, chart components, or data models.
- Reduces reliance on IT teams by giving admins tools to self-diagnose issues.

  •  For End Users (Managers, HRBPs, etc.):

- Ensures faster access to performance-critical reports and improves data-driven decision-making ensuring reports load reliably.
- Leads to a better user experience with less waiting time and smoother navigation.

  • Overall Organizational Benefits:

- Saves time and effort by eliminating guesswork around report performance issues.
- Helps maintain reporting integrity and reliability in strategic HR processes like performance management, compensation reviews.

What You’ll Need:-

For successful utilization of this enhancement, make sure:

  • You have access to Stories in Report Center.
  • You have the Role-Based Permission Prerequisites: - Administrator > Manage Dashboards/Reports > Story Admin

Now let’s walk through the Procedure to utilize the Story Performance Analysis Tool: -

Step 1.  To access the Performance Analysis Tool, choose Story Performance Analysis Tool from the Report Center.

 

Landing Page of the Performance Analysis Tool:-

Step 2.  To set the date range:

  1. Click the Session Date token.
  2. Adjust the existing range or add a new range.

Step 3.  To apply additional filters:

  1. Click on User or Resource
  2. Select the appropriate member(s).

Step 4.  In the list of results, click to select the report/item to analyze.

 

The report is analyzed in 3 sections namely -

  • Summary
  • Details
  • History

a.) Summary Section

The Summary section provides a top-level overview of the total performance footprint of the Story report. It captures the end-to-end load time, determine where high-level delays are occurring and distributes it across the 3 primary system layers involved in report execution:

 Network – Time spent in data transfer between the server and the client

 Backend – Time taken to retrieve and prepare data from the data model

Frontend – Time taken to render charts, tables, and visuals on the user interface.

- Under Summary section,

  • Startup Time displays – The Timespan from the initiation of the Actions "Open" and "Go to Page to the finalized page.
  • Total Widget Time displays – The sum of all usage times for each Widget, including multiple uses.
  • Maximum Backend Duration displays – The maximum backend processing time per model, noting totals may rise with multiple widgets or refreshes. 
  • Maximum Widget Time and Total Widget Time displays – Maximum and median processing times per layer (frontend, backend, network) across all widgets during user interactions

 

b.) Details Section

The Details section provides a granular breakdown of performance data, offering visibility into how specific report components contribute to overall load time. This includes: - Performance of individual pages within the Story, Load time of specific widgets, such as charts, graphs, or tables, Execution time for backend queries, including filtering, sorting, and data transformation

It is used by admins to troubleshoot performance bottlenecks and identifies exact sources of delay, facilitates targeted optimizations, reduces trial-and-error during report tuning.

- Under Details section,

  • Page Load Time displays – Total load time per story page, split by phase, with peak backend, frontend, and network times
  • Widget Drilldown displays – Widget load time with action, page, model, and backend request details.
  • Loading Sequence displays - Widget load time by action, split into backend, network, and frontend

 

c.) History Section

The History section records a time-stamped log of previous performance analyses conducted on the same Story report. It enables comparison of performance metrics across different test runs or after configuration changes.

It supports trend analysis and performance benchmarking over time and tracks performance improvements or regressions, provides historical evidence of tuning efforts, supports continuous monitoring for business-critical reports

Under History section,

  • Total Time per Date displays – Charts compare a user's performance history with others, switching measures based on filters like page, action, or widget.
  • Runtime Split per Date displays – Charts compare a single user’s runtime performance (median backend, network, frontend times) against all users, independent of filters.

 

Step 5.  Take Action Based on Insights: -

  1. Optimize charts and pages with high frontend time.
  2. Adjust or reduce filters and calculations affecting backend time.
  3. Collaborate with your technical team if network issues persist.

Administrators/Users also have the option –

  • To enable the Show in Milliseconds toggle which will display the Time in milliseconds
  • To apply filters as per the requirement

You've reached the end of this process!

With minimal effort, administrators can directly access Story Performance Analysis Tool within Report Center, enhancing ability to assess and optimize the efficiency of Story reports and identifying areas for improvement and optimize report performance.

I hope this blog added value to your learning journey. Feel free to like, share your thoughts in the comments, and spread the word! Keep learning and growing.

#1H2025Release  #Stories  #People Analytics #SAP SuccessFactors

entry.title: Customizing LMS user profile: Showing Full Cost Center Details in One field
entry.content:
"I previously came across an issue where the client requested the Cost Center field to be included in the user profile within LMS. They want both the Cost Center ID and Cost Center description to appear in a single field." I am writing this blog to assist scenarios where clients require multiple data elements to be displayed within a single field. For example: If the client has the requirement to concatenate other fields like First name and Last name etc. so this approach will be useful in all the cases.

 Issue Overview

The client has requested the inclusion of the Cost Center field in the user profile within the SAP SuccessFactors Learning Management System (LMS). Specifically, they require both the Cost Center ID and its description to be displayed together in a single field. This request presents a challenge as LMS typically handles such data in separate fields or relies on specific formatting or configuration for combined values. The issue involves determining a feasible way to capture and display both pieces of information within the limitations of the LMS user profile structure.

Issue Identified:
The LMS user profile currently does not support displaying both the Cost Center ID and Cost Center description in a single field by default. This limitation makes it difficult to meet the client's requirement of combining both values for better clarity and reporting. A custom approach or configuration may be needed to concatenate and display both elements in one field within the LMS.

 

  1. Troubleshooting Steps

I followed a step-by-step approach to diagnose and fix the problem:

Step 1: Verify Source Fields Availability

  • Action Taken:
    Confirm that both Cost Center (ID) and Cost Center name (Description) fields are available in the Corporate data model.

Step 2: Open the Integration Center Job

  • Action Taken:

Navigate to Admin Center > Integration Center > My Integrations.
Open the existing LMS user data export integration job

Step 3: Add Cost Center Field

  • Action Taken:

Use the "Insert Fields" tab in the integration builder to add the field
Click "Add" button and named the field as “CUSTOM01”

Step 4: Create a Calculated Field

  • Action Taken:
    In the Integration Center job:
    Add a calculation on the new field.

Find Field Starting from Employment details and Add association Selected Field
Use Concatenate function to combine Cost Center (name) and Cost Center (Code)
Example format attached below:

Step 5: Preview data and Save the Job

  • Action Taken:

Use the Preview tab in Integration Center to ensure the concatenated output appears correctly for test users.
Go to the Save button and Click on Save

Step 6: Map the Calculated Field to LMS

  • Action Taken:
    Identify an appropriate custom field in LMS (e.g., field number 10) and give the name like COST CENTER that can store the combined Cost Center data.

Navigate to Learning Administration > Custom Fields > User

 

  • Map the newly created calculated field to that LMS field in the CONNECTOR Configuration

Navigate to Learning Administration > Configuration> System Configuration > Connectors

Step 7: Run the Integration Job (Test Mode)

  • Action Taken:
    Run the integration in test mode with a few sample records.
    Ensure the custom field in LMS is updated with the combined Cost Center value.

Step 8: Verify in LMS

  • Action Taken:
    Log in to the LMS admin interface and navigate to the user profile.
    Confirm that the custom field (e.g., Custom01) displays the Cost Center in the desired format (ID + Description).
  • Note: If the result does not match the client’s expected format, update the calculation logic in Integration Center accordingly.

Step 9: Deploy to Production

  • Action Taken:
    Once tested successfully, schedule or deploy the integration job to production.
    Monitor the first run for data accuracy and integrity.

entry.title: Standard Site URL in RMK
entry.content:
In this blog I would like to talk about one of the recent release of recruitment marketing "Standard Site URL" which benefits customers to no longer maintain DNS and purchase SSL.

Standard Site URL in RMK: 

As we all know that SSL certificate ensures secure communication between career site and recruiting application. Maintaining SSL certificate is mandatory for any production instance to progress with the go live of CSB.

Till this release for any implementation, we create the SSL certificate in two ways details below:

SSL Certificates Options

The page displays two options you can use to install SSL:

  • Option 1 where you fill out a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) form that you use to obtain an SSL certificate from a certificate authority.
  • Option 2 allows you to upload an SSL certificate that you obtained using your own CSR.

To further understand the above process of maintaining SSL click Here

SAP has introduced a new release along with the above process by providing clients with an alternative option called “Standard Site URLs for Career Sites”.

By using this release update, clients can now choose to use a standard URL for your career site with a generic SAP domain, so that customers no longer have to maintain the DNS and SSL certificate because SAP maintains DNS and SSL certificate for the site.

Example - <subdomain>.jobs.hr.cloud.sap, where subdomain is unique to its organization.

When creating the sub domain, one can always check its availability by clicking on the button “Check Availability” shown in the picture below making sure that the subdomain can be used for the site url.

Procedure to create standard site URL:

To configure a standard site URL in Career Site Builder, go to Settings > Site Configuration and open the Site URL tab.

The process of transitioning from a custom site URL to a standard site URL. Refer to the points below.

  1. Configure the Standard Site URL and save.
  2. If you use an internal career site powered by Career Site Builder, update the SAML 2.0 configuration in your identity provider so that internal candidates can access the site at the new URL with single sign-on.
  3. Select the standard URL as Primary and save. All career site functionality now uses the standard URL with the SAP domain.
  4. Continue to maintain your secondary, custom URL during the transition period, while you manage the change within your organization. The custom URL remains active as long as the SSL certificate is valid. (The SSL Certificates tool in Career Site Builder only applies to the custom URL, whether it's primary or secondary.
  5. Plan to complete the transition within a 6-month period:
    • When the secondary custom URL is no longer needed, you can let the SSL certificate expire and remove it from the Custom Site URL field at any time.
    • After 6 months, the secondary custom URL stops redirecting automatically. To extend the transition period, you need to set up the redirection yourself and continue to maintain the SSL certificates.

Current view for configuring standard URL

To facilitate a transition from 1 URL to the other, you can maintain 2 active URLs for up to 6 months, 1 primary and 1 secondary. By default, your existing custom URL is selected as Primary. The primary URL is your site's true web address and the domain on which all other functionality is based, such as job alerts, email notifications, and integrations. The secondary URL redirects automatically to the primary URL, for up to 6 months.

The 6-month transition period starts whenever you change the Primary URL selection. When you do, other configurations that use the site URL, like job alert emails or integration settings, are automatically updated to reflect the new primary URL.

After 6 months, the secondary URL is removed automatically from the site configuration in Career Site Builder and no longer redirects automatically.

Note:

After opting for Standard URL, verify if there is any need to update the back link or site URLs maintained in the company portal or any other Corporate site if any exists.

Conclusion: 

Using this new release clients can benefit by not buying SSL and maintain DNS for the career site.

entry.title: How IAS and IPS Help Secure SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Hey SAP Community! If you're like me, you're always trying to make business apps easier and safer to use. Today, I want to chat about two tools that help with that: Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and Identity Provisioning Service (IPS). They’re big players in SAP, especially for managing users in SAP SuccessFactors. But what do they actually do, and why should you care? Let’s break it down!

What is IAS?

Think of IAS like the front door to your apps. It makes sure only the right people get in. IAS helps you by letting users log in once and access everything they need without logging in again (that's called single sign-on or SSO). Plus, you get to pick how users log in, whether it’s with a password, a social media account, or even using two steps for extra security.

Cool Things IAS Can Do:

  • Easy Login (SSO): Users love not having to log in over and over on different apps. It's super convenient.
  • Extra Security: You can require more login checks based on who the user is or where they are logging in from. This keeps your apps safe.
  • Works with Other Services: Got Microsoft Azure Active Directory? No worries. IAS can talk to these outside services so everything works together smoothly.
  • Manage Setup with API: Using the tech behind IAS, you spend less time tweaking settings and more time using your apps.

IAS is like the guard at the entrance, keeping everything secure without making it complicated.

Getting to Know IPS

Now onto IPS! It’s not just a fancy name. If IAS is the front door, IPS is the key that makes sure everyone has the right access. It makes managing user identities simple across both cloud systems and systems running internally on your company's hardware.

Cool Things IPS Can Do:

  • User Management: IPS jumps in after you're set up with IAS to ensure everyone gets the right access on all platforms. Think of it like your personal assistant for user access.
  • Data Control: You don’t need all data everywhere. With IPS, you can control what goes where and tailor it to your needs.
  • Transparency and Notifications: IPS keeps you informed with logs and notifications about what's happening with user provisioning. You always know what's going on.

 

 

 

IPS works quietly behind the scenes, giving users access smoothly without manual hassle.

Why IAS and IPS Matter

You might be thinking, "Do I really need these services?" Absolutely, yes! SAP wants to make sure identity authentication is simple and pre-configured in their systems. This means less worry about user access.

IAS can also manage other identity providers, integrating them easily into your workflows. You get strong security and ease in one package.

Setting Up IAS/IPS

Wondering how to set this up? You’re not alone. IAS/IPS comes with many SAP cloud services, but you can also set them up through SAP Business Technology Platform. The best place to start is SAP's Tenant Discovery link to see if you have them ready.

Customizing and Using IAS/IPS

After setup, you can customize login pages, email templates, and usage terms. Check out SAP’s help documents for detailed guides on making the most of IAS/IPS:

  • Identity Authentication Help Documents
  • Identity Provisioning Help Documents

 

Wrapping Up

So there you have it, a simple look at why IAS and IPS are key for SAP SuccessFactors and other SAP cloud systems. They make life easier for you and your users, ensuring smooth and secure access. I hope this helps you understand these great tools better. Until next time, keep your identities safe and your systems secure!
